tagline: Random forests adapted to estimate conditional average treatment effects τ(x) = E[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0) | X_i = x] rather than a single ATE.
selection_problem: Even under unconfoundedness, a naive difference E[Y|D=1,X] - E[Y|D=0,X] estimates τ(x) only if the model is correctly specified. Causal forests nonparametrically target the CATE without imposing a functional form on how treatment effects vary with X.
counterfactual_problem: For each treated unit with covariates X_i = x, we never observe Y_i(0), the outcome they would have had without treatment at that exact covariate value.
po_setup: Y_i(1) is the outcome under treatment, Y_i(0) under control, D_i is treatment assignment. The estimand is the CATE τ(x) = E[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0) | X_i = x], which varies across the covariate space.

estimand_math.label: Assumption
estimand_math.expr: Y_i(0), Y_i(1) independent of D_i given X_i (unconfoundedness)

po_intuition: A single regression coefficient hides that τ(x) may be large for some subgroups and zero or negative for others. The PO framework forces you to ask: for whom is the effect large, and does your comparison group share the same X_i distribution.
solution: Causal forests build trees that partition the covariate space to maximize heterogeneity in treatment effects, then use a nearest-neighbor-in-leaf average to estimate τ(x). Nearby units in covariate space serve as counterfactuals for each other.
key_insight: By using honest estimation — splitting the sample into tree-building and estimation halves — causal forests achieve asymptotically normal, pointwise-valid confidence intervals for τ(x) despite the high-dimensional nonparametric search.

assumptions.name: Unconfoundedness
assumptions.formal: D_i independent of (Y_i(0), Y_i(1)) given X_i
assumptions.intuition: If unobserved confounders drive treatment, the within-leaf comparisons are still apples-to-oranges and τ(x) estimates are biased.

assumptions.name: Overlap
assumptions.formal: η ≤ P(D_i=1 | X_i=x) ≤ 1-η for some η > 0
assumptions.intuition: Leaves with no treated or no control units cannot form a local difference-in-means, so extreme propensity scores destroy identification.

example_setup: A state rolls out a job-training voucher program; eligibility is determined by a complex rule based on age, education, and prior earnings that makes assignment as-good-as-random conditional on those covariates. The outcome is earnings two years later.
example_counterfactual: For each worker who used a voucher, we cannot observe what their earnings would have been in the exact same labor-market conditions without the training.
example_execution: The causal forest partitions workers into leaves where age, education, and prior earnings are similar, then computes the local treatment-control earnings gap within each leaf. τ(x) is estimated separately for each worker's covariate profile.
example_result: Young workers with low prior earnings show τ(x) ≈ +$4,200/year, while older high-earners show τ(x) ≈ +$300/year, suggesting strong effect heterogeneity by baseline human capital.
source_title: Estimation and Inference of Heterogeneous Treatment Effects using Random Forests

math_panel.label: Honesty gain
math_panel.expr: Bias → 0 as n → ∞ under honest splitting
math_panel.note: Enables valid CIs for each τ(x)

dag_shape.caption: Unconfoundedness requires U has no arrow into D after conditioning on X. Causal forest estimates τ(x) locally within regions of the X space.
